item.title: When you're nice to the weird girl and now you have 2
item.description: After seeing my wife put on her unicorn onesie, my daughter demanded her dragon robe. This is my life. Unicorns and Dragons... And I wouldn't have it any other way

item.title: Just let me die in peace
item.description: I dodged it for 2.5 years, but it finally caught up with me. Yes, I'm vaxxed. The advice from an unvaccinated family friend who just caught it for like the 4th time: take ivermectin or hydroxychloroquin. /facepalm EDIT: This post made Most Viral! ? The irony!

item.title: Recording Sessions from Behind the Scenes of Disney Classics
item.description:
Brad Kane and Lea Salonga - Aladdin
Jodi Benson - The Little Mermaid
Illene Woods - Cinderella
Ed Wynn, Jerry Colonna and Kathryn Beaumont - Alice in Wonderland
Judy Kuhn - Pocahontas
Peggy Lee - Lady and the Tramp
Tom Hanks and Tim Allen - Toy Story
Eartha Kitt - a deleted song from The Emperor's New Groove
A recording session from 1940, featuring Walt Disney himself as Mickey and character actor Billy Bletcher as Pete
Lillias White, Cheryl Freeman, LaChanze, Roz Ryan and Vanéese Y. Thomas (The Muses) - Hercules
The cast of The Lion King
Angela Lansbury and Jerry Orbach - Beauty and the Beast
Louie Prima - The Jungle Book
Robin Williams recording voice over bits for an old MGM Studios attraction (The Magic of Animation which toured the then working Florida animation studio). He also starred in a 1989 short film with Walter Cronkite that played at the start of the tour - https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2PbUGTZdRjM
Lea Salonga - Mulan
Mandy Moore - Tangled
Tim Curry - FernGully: The Last Rainforest
Susan Egan - Hercules
Phil Collins recording in multiple languages for Tarzan
Thurl Ravenscroft and other actors recording Grim Grinning Ghosts for the Haunted Mansion, as the singing busts in the cemetary (a trick illusion projects their faces onto fake heads).
Jason Alexander and Mary Wickes - The Hunchback of Notre Dame
More from the choir and other orchestra of The Hunchback of Notre Dame
item.dc:creator: Pasquinel

item.title: It's Midsummer in Sweden today so here's a small Swedish dump!
item.description:
Yep, that's what we do
Nubbe is like a shot of spicy booze
It's a joke in Sweden that it's always raining on Midsummer.
I had my first glas of wine about an hour ago
Hehe, we're all mother pigs ?
Finlands celebration like looks quite dope
As tradition I made my own flower crown! Happy Friday guys! And Swedes; jump over some fences, pick 7 different flowers, get drunk and make some babies! GLAD MIDSOMMAR! https://imgur.com/a/IdWzhxF
item.dc:creator: Miiaka

item.title: Parable of Harry R Truman
item.description:
Harry R Truman was a the mold of a 20th century badass. Harry was a Veteran of the first World War, a private businessman, and an entrepreneur. His sister remembered him as "A very opinionated man." He held fast to unshakable convictions. I made this post because when I learned about the history of the 20th century, there were a lot of parts that my elementary school history book left out, and Mr. Truman was one of them. Harry was rarely seen without his glass of whiskey and Coca-Cola. "I don't like to drink" he said. "People drive me to it."
Harry R. Truman owned the Spirit Lake lodge at the foot of Mount St. Helens, and had owned it for decades. Over 50 years the lodge had been destroyed by weather twice, and twice Harry built it back again. Harry was the living embodiment of the American dream and pulling yourself up by your bootstraps. He ran the business purely on his own effort an ingenuity. He famously said several times that if you didn't care for the way he ran his business, you were welcome to see Mt. St. Helens somewhere else. "If you don't like my apples, don't shake my tree." he said.
The area owned and operated by Mr. Truman was one of the most famously beautiful areas in the Mt. St. Helens scenic area. Built on Spirit Lake right at the foot of the great mountain, the nature, views, and atmosphere were unparalleled. The area was remote and difficult to access, and Harry navigated the challenge of building and running a business far, far off the beaten path. When Harry first arrived, he had to get to his lodge by snowshoe. The Spirit Lake Lodge owned by Harry R. Truman was the quintessential American experience. Owned by a veteran and good ol' boy, it was one of the best ways to see the beauty of the untamed Pacific Northwest.
However, as most reading already know, not everything stayed calm and tranquil at Spirit lake. In March 1980, Mt. St. Helens abruptly reminded America that the mountain was in fact a stratovolcano, and it was still very much alive. A series of earthquakes tracked by the United States Geological Survey (USGS) quickly made it clear that magma was moving inside the volcano. Mt. St. Helens was no longer dormant. An eruption was coming. The data collected by USGS made it clear it was not a question of if, only a question of when the mountain would have a large, major eruption. The governor declared a state of emergency. the USGS quickly defined a "red zone" within which the population would be at risk because of the approaching eruption. The area was closed. Landowners were evacuated. Except Harry R. Truman. Who made it clear he wasn't going anywhere.
This is the part I feel like history books often leave out. Private landowners and weathy vactioners with private cabins were incensed at the governor closing the area. Protests built at police blockades all around the mountain. The people wanted back in. Many people wanted the opportunity to at least go in and recover some of their property. Others simply wanted to go vacation as normal and were angry that the closures interrupted their ability to visit their own property.
Overnight, Harry R. Truman became an American Folk Hero. A war veteran that took no shit, Harry wasn't moving. Despite repeated requests from the authorities and the USGS, Harry refused to evacuate. This caused chaos for the authorities. Not only was Harry in danger, but news crews kept putting themselves in danger sneaking into the red zone for a chance to interview the famous Harry R. Truman. Perhaps his most famous moment, Harry told one such news broadcast "That mountain and that lake is apart of Truman and I'm a part of it." He became the rallying banner for local landowners to go around local safety efforts, and a story that the American media and American citizens loved. Harry was the living embodiment US stick-to-it-iveness. Not only wasn't he leaving, he made it clear to anyone that would listen that he didn't think the eruption was going to be as bad as predicted. He resented people attempting to show him the evidence of the scientists working at the USGS. "...the mountain has shot its wad and it hasn't hurt my place a bit, but those goddamn geologists with their hair down to their butts wouldn't pay no attention to ol' Truman." Harry believed that even if there was an eruption, the local timber forests and the lake would protect him and his property from any damage.
Harry was wrong. The morning of May 18th, the side of Mt. St. Helens slid away in the largest landslide in recorded history. This released pressure on several magma chambers. Over approximately 1 minute, Mt. St. Helens exploded with the power of 1600 Hiroshima bombs.
A wave of explosive gas expanded out from the blast faster than the speed of sound. In mere moments, the blast obliterated Spirit Lodge, buried the entire area in multiple square kilometers of rock and magma, and then the entire area was covered in water displaced from the lake.
The trees that Truman thought would protect him were blown over like grass. So many trees were displaced that lakes in the area are still covered with the dead timber TO THIS DAY.
My point is that Harry R. Truman was a dipshit. I don't mind that he was 80 years old and he made the decision to die with his lodge. That I respect and good for him. He got what he wanted. My problem is he told anyone that would listen that the scientists didn't know what they were talking about and it was going to be fine. This ended up on the news all over the country. Here's the thing, he in fact was not a scientist and had not a clue what he was talking about. And he fucking died. We've had this story go around and around and around in the United States. Energy, Covid, Climate Change. Turns out scientific consensus matters and bad things do in fact happen to dumb people. It bugs me that people still think of Harry R. Truman as a hero and a man to be admired. He absolutely wasn't that. He was a stubborn old man that spewed misinformation, and we ate it up. It sucks that there is stuff in the area named in his honor, he has a big monument to him. If you want a hero, look to the local USGS scientists that gave their lives to protect American citizens. Look up and remember David A. Johnston who died to give early warning to local authories. Next time a dumb guy gets a microphone, remember that that person is an early-stage Harry R. Truman
item.dc:creator: dogfromthehills

item.title: Simplified Paper Enigma for decoding numbers
item.description: I’ve been playing with the Paper Enigma idea, originally created by Michael C. Koss. This is a much more simplified version based on his original Paper Enigma design. I’ve made this numbered version that only has one reflector, one main disk and two key numbers. For this I had to redraw the paths and connect them all around. It works! It’s a fun little puzzle that I would like to make out of better materials one day. This is a lame demonstration of how it works.
item.dc:creator: PaulDeGraaf
